content.text: are range estimates that quantify uncertainty in statistical estimates by providing a range of plausible values for an unknown population parameter (like mean, proportion, or model accuracy) along with a specified confidence level. Instead of reporting a single point estimate (e.g., "model accuracy = 85%"), a confidence interval gives a range (e.g., "accuracy = 85% ± 2%, or [83%, 87%]") with 95% confidence, telling you how precise and reliable your estimate is.
What it is — Clear definition and intuition
for parameter θ is an interval [L, U] where:
: If you repeat the experiment 100 times and compute a 95% CI each time,
95 of those intervals will contain the true parameter
: "There's a 95% probability that the true value is in this specific interval".
The true parameter θ is fixed (not random); the interval bounds vary across samples. The confidence level describes the procedure's reliability, not the probability for a specific interval.

Imagine fishing for a parameter:

True value: θ = 100 (fixed, underwater)

Sample 1: CI = [95, 105] → Contains θ (caught!)
Sample 2: CI = [102, 110] → Doesn't contain θ (missed!)
Sample 3: CI = [98, 103] → Contains θ (caught!)
...
After 100 samples with 95% CI: 95 intervals contain θ, 5 don't

The 95% is about the PROCEDURE, not any single interval [web:249][web:253]
Why it matters — Importance in Generative AI
Confidence intervals are essential for quantifying uncertainty in ML:
Report accuracy as 85% ± 2% (95% CI) instead of just 85%
CI shows how precise predictions are
CI for difference in model performance shows precision of estimate
CI on error rates ensures safety requirements are met with high confidence
PPI/CRC methods generate reliable CIs for metrics using LLM annotations
CONffusion constructs CIs around each pixel for safe generation
: When reporting GPT-4 accuracy as "85% (95% CI: [83%, 87%])", you're saying the true accuracy is plausibly between 83% and 87%, and this method would capture the true value 95% of the time.
= critical value from Normal distribution (e.g., z = 1.96 for 95% CI)
For mean (μ) with unknown σ (small sample):
= critical value from t-distribution with df = n-1

Measured accuracy over 100 samples: Ȳ = 0.85
Population σ = 0.05 (known)
n = 100
95% CI: z = 1.96

Margin of error = 1.96 × (0.05 / √100)
                = 1.96 × (0.05 / 10)
                = 1.96 × 0.005
                = 0.0098

Lower = 0.85 - 0.0098 = 0.8402
Upper = 0.85 + 0.0098 = 0.8598

95% CI = [0.840, 0.860]

Interpretation: True accuracy is between 84.0% and 86.0% with 95% confidence.

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# Sample data
data = np.array([10, 12, 15, 11, 13])

# Point estimate
mean = np.mean(data)
std = np.std(data, ddof=1)  # sample std
n = len(data)

# 95% CI using t-distribution
alpha = 0.05
t_critical = stats.t.ppf(1 - alpha/2, df=n-1)
margin_of_error = t_critical * (std / np.sqrt(n))

ci_lower = mean - margin_of_error
ci_upper = mean + margin_of_error

print(f"Mean: {mean:.2f}")
print(f"95% CI: [{ci_lower:.2f}, {ci_upper:.2f}]")
# Mean: 12.20, 95% CI: [9.81, 14.59]

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# 85 correct out of 100
p_hat = 85 / 100  # 0.85
n = 100

# 95% CI using Normal approximation
alpha = 0.05
z_critical = stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha/2)  # 1.96
margin_of_error = z_critical * np.sqrt(p_hat * (1 - p_hat) / n)

ci_lower = p_hat - margin_of_error
ci_upper = p_hat + margin_of_error

print(f"Accuracy: {p_hat:.2f}")
print(f"95% CI: [{ci_lower:.3f}, {ci_upper:.3f}]")
# Accuracy: 0.85, 95% CI: [0.774, 0.926]

import numpy as np

# Sample data
data = np.array([10, 12, 15, 11, 13])

# Bootstrap: repeatedly sample and compute mean
bootstrap_means = []
for i in range(10000):
    bootstrap_sample = np.random.choice(data, size=len(data), replace=True)
    bootstrap_means.append(np.mean(bootstrap_sample))

# 95% CI using percentile method
ci_lower = np.percentile(bootstrap_means, 2.5)  # 2.5th percentile
ci_upper = np.percentile(bootstrap_means, 97.5)  # 97.5th percentile

print(f"95% Bootstrap CI: [{ci_lower:.2f}, {ci_upper:.2f}]")

A 95% CI includes the null hypothesis value if p ≥ 0.05
A 95% CI excludes the null hypothesis value if p < 0.05 [web:202][web:206]

: Testing if Model A > Model B (H₀: difference = 0)
- If CI for difference = [0.02, 0.08]: Excludes 0 → p < 0.05 → Reject H₀
- If CI for difference = [-0.01, 0.05]: Includes 0 → p ≥ 0.05 → Fail to reject H₀
Why CI is better than p-value alone:
Shows how large the effect is
Easy ("true value is between X and Y")
Confidence intervals provide more information about
"95% chance true value is in CI"
CI is about the procedure: 95% of such intervals contain θ, not probability for specific interval
CI includes null value → insufficient evidence, not proof of no effect
Use t-distribution when n < 30 and σ unknown/12:
Small samples → wider CI; report CI to show precision
Computing many CIs increases false positives; adjust confidence level
A point estimate ("accuracy = 85%") gives no sense of how reliable it is. A CI ([83%, 87%]) immediately communicates precision — a crucial distinction when making deployment decisions for ML models.

Unlike a p-value which only says "significant/not significant", a CI shows whether the effect is large enough to matter practically (e.g., a [+0.1%, +0.3%] improvement in model accuracy may be statistically significant but practically irrelevant).
Engineers can use CIs to ask whether a new model is definitively better: if the 95% CI for accuracy difference excludes zero, you can confidently deploy the new model.
The CI naturally widens for small samples and narrows for large ones, automatically encoding the intuition that small experiments are less conclusive.
Bootstrap CIs require no distributional assumption:
When the sampling distribution is unknown (custom metrics, complex estimators), bootstrap CIs provide reliable intervals without assuming normality.
CIs from multiple independent experiments can be combined via inverse-variance weighting to produce a pooled estimate — not possible with p-values alone.

The correct interpretation ("95% of such intervals contain θ") is counterintuitive. Bayesian credible intervals ("there is 95% probability θ is in [L, U]") are easier to communicate but require a prior.
Multiple comparisons inflate false discovery rate:
Computing 20 independent 95% CIs, one is expected to exclude the true value by chance. Apply Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg correction when testing many hypotheses simultaneously.
Wide CIs for small samples are often uninformative:
A 95% CI of [60%, 95%] for model accuracy provides almost no useful constraint. CIs only become informative at sample sizes typically above 100–200 per metric.
Normal approximation fails for small samples and extreme proportions:
The standard proportion CI uses a normal approximation that breaks down when
. Use Wilson or Clopper-Pearson intervals instead.
10,000 bootstrap resamples are typically needed for stable 95% CIs; this is computationally expensive for complex estimators on large datasets.
When to Use Each CI Type
Rare in practice; σ seldom known
Normal approx. unreliable for small n or extreme p
Widen individual CIs to maintain family-wise coverage
Real-World Example: A/B Testing Model Versions

A production ML team wants to compare two ranking models. Model A scores 0.782 and Model B scores 0.791 on NDCG@10 over 400 test queries. They need to determine if the difference is statistically reliable before incurring the cost of retraining and redeploying.

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# Simulate per-query NDCG@10 scores for 400 queries
ndcg_a = rng.beta(7, 3, 400)     # mean ~0.70
ndcg_b = rng.beta(7.5, 3, 400)   # mean ~0.71 (slightly better)

# Paired t-test: each query is a matched pair (same queries for both models)
diff = ndcg_b - ndcg_a
n = len(diff)
mean_diff = diff.mean()
se_diff = diff.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(n)
t_crit = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=n - 1)

ci_lo = mean_diff - t_crit * se_diff
ci_hi = mean_diff + t_crit * se_diff

print(f"NDCG@10 difference (B - A): {mean_diff:+.4f}")
print(f"95% CI: [{ci_lo:+.4f}, {ci_hi:+.4f}]")
print(f"CI excludes zero: {ci_lo > 0 or ci_hi < 0}")

# Decision logic
if ci_lo > 0:
    print("=> Model B is significantly better — safe to deploy.")
elif ci_hi < 0:
    print("=> Model A is significantly better — revert.")
else:
    print("=> No significant difference — collect more data or deploy either.")

# Bootstrap CI for robustness check
n_boot = 10_000
boot_diffs = [np.mean(rng.choice(diff, n, replace=True)) for _ in range(n_boot)]
boot_lo = np.percentile(boot_diffs, 2.5)
boot_hi = np.percentile(boot_diffs, 97.5)
print(f"\nBootstrap 95% CI: [{boot_lo:+.4f}, {boot_hi:+.4f}]")

Standard CI formulas are O(n) to compute (one pass to compute mean and standard deviation). Bootstrap CIs are O(n · B) where B is the number of bootstrap resamples (typically 10,000). For very large datasets this can be expensive.

attrs.latex: T_{\text{analytic CI}} = O(n) \qquad T_{\text{bootstrap CI}} = O(n \cdot B) \qquad T_{\text{BCA bootstrap}} = O(n \cdot B + n^2)

content.text:
import numpy as np
import time

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
data = rng.exponential(scale=2, size=10_000)   # non-normal data

# Analytic t-CI
t0 = time.perf_counter()
from scipy import stats
ci_analytic = stats.t.interval(0.95, df=len(data)-1,
                                loc=data.mean(),
                                scale=stats.sem(data))
t_analytic = time.perf_counter() - t0

# Bootstrap CI (vectorised using numpy)
t0 = time.perf_counter()
B = 10_000
boot_means = rng.choice(data, (B, len(data)), replace=True).mean(axis=1)
ci_boot = (np.percentile(boot_means, 2.5), np.percentile(boot_means, 97.5))
t_boot = time.perf_counter() - t0

print(f"Analytic t-CI:   {ci_analytic} in {t_analytic*1000:.2f}ms")
print(f"Bootstrap 95%CI: {tuple(f'{x:.4f}' for x in ci_boot)} in {t_boot*1000:.1f}ms")
computes Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and normal approximation CIs for proportions.
for comparing two sample means with CIs.
returning effect size, power, and CIs in a single call — convenient for model comparison reports.
Fast bootstrap CI computation with support for custom statistics functions and Bayesian bootstrap.
Bayesian credible intervals via MCMC sampling;
computes highest density intervals from posterior samples — the Bayesian analogue of CIs.

# scipy bootstrap CI (scipy >= 1.7)
from scipy.stats import bootstrap
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
data = rng.lognormal(0, 1, 200)

# Custom statistic: 90th percentile
result = bootstrap(
    (data,),
    statistic=lambda x: np.percentile(x, 90),
    confidence_level=0.95,
    n_resamples=10_000,
    random_state=42,
)
print(f"90th percentile: {np.percentile(data, 90):.3f}")
print(f"95% CI: [{result.confidence_interval.low:.3f}, {result.confidence_interval.high:.3f}]")

# Wilson CI for proportions (better than normal approx for small samples)
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint

n, k = 50, 5   # 5 successes out of 50 trials (p=10%)
lo_normal, hi_normal = proportion_confint(k, n, method="normal")
lo_wilson, hi_wilson = proportion_confint(k, n, method="wilson")
print(f"\nNormal CI:  [{lo_normal:.3f}, {hi_normal:.3f}]")
print(f"Wilson CI:  [{lo_wilson:.3f}, {hi_wilson:.3f}]  ← prefer for small n")
Range containing θ with posterior probability (1-α)
When prior knowledge is available; easier communication
Probability of data at least as extreme given H₀
Hypothesis testing; does not show effect size
Std dev of the sampling distribution of estimator
Average spread of estimates across samples
CI construction; uncertainty in parameter estimates
Range for a new individual observation
New obs will fall here with (1-α) probability
Forecasting; wider than CI (adds irreducible noise)
Range containing at least proportion p of population
(1-α) CI for the p-th quantile range
Statistical inference has no inherent security surface, but several reliability and ethical risks arise when confidence intervals are used in production ML systems.

Publishing only statistically significant CI results (where CI excludes zero) while discarding others inflates the apparent improvement rate. Pre-register experiments with hypothesis, sample size, and primary metric before collecting data.
If the same data is used for model training and test-set CI computation, the CI underestimates true uncertainty. Use strict train/validation/test splits and compute CIs only on held-out test data.

A CI computed on test data from Q1 may not reflect model performance in Q4 if the data distribution shifts. Compute rolling CIs on recent data windows and trigger retraining alerts when the CI for key metrics shifts.
A 99% accuracy CI sounds impressive but may hide a model that predicts the majority class for everything. Always compute CIs on per-class metrics (precision, recall) in addition to overall accuracy.
In A/B tests comparing many model variants simultaneously, unadjusted 95% CIs will produce false positives at rate 1 - (0.95)^K. Apply Bonferroni (
: If you repeat experiment 100 times, 95 intervals will contain true parameter
: "95% of intervals contain θ" ≠ "95% chance θ is in this interval"
: CI includes null value if p ≥ 0.05; excludes if p < 0.05
: Width indicates uncertainty; narrower = more precise
: Use when no distribution assumption (percentile method)

For Generative AI, confidence intervals quantify uncertainty in model predictions, report performance metrics with precision, validate safety requirements, and provide statistical backing for claims about model quality in production.
